text: 3.5g of fat. cholesterol, saturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat are doing most of the work. for dairy, the fat side can either add richness or drag the whole thing down.
4.8g of carbs. sugar and glycemic index matter most here. for a dairy food, that is a strong sign.
3.5g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits here.
vitamin b12 at 12% daily value stands out most. that is barely moving the needle.
calcium at 10% daily value stands out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

pros first: fermented dairy practicality is a real plus. it keeps the category strengths while usually being a bit more interesting in practice. clear calcium identity. that is a core category strength. easy protein delivery for normal diets. dairy gets real credit for convenience.

on the weaker side: processing changes the category a lot. the label alone does not tell the whole story. some versions get dense fast on fat, sodium, or both. the category swings a lot by format. not everyone tolerates dairy well. that is a real use-case limitation.
A tier. Best when the protein payoff or fermentation angle outweighs the fat and sodium tradeoffs.
